questions.q: What is the primary difference between HTTP and HTTPS?
options: HTTPS encrypts data using TLS/SSL while HTTP transmits data in plaintext, making HTTPS secure for sensitive information
HTTP is faster because it does not encrypt, while HTTPS is slower
HTTPS uses TCP while HTTP uses UDP
HTTP is used only for web pages while HTTPS is used for all internet traffic
questions.explanation: HTTPS wraps HTTP in a TLS/SSL layer, encrypting the entire communication channel between client and server. This protects against eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks. Both use TCP. Both can serve any type of content; HTTPS is now the standard for security-conscious applications.

questions.q: Which HTTP method is used to request data from a server without any side effects?
options: GET retrieves data without modifying resources on the server
POST submits data to the server and may modify state
PUT replaces an entire resource at a specified URI
DELETE removes a resource and returns the deleted data
questions.explanation: GET is an idempotent, safe method for retrieving data. POST creates or modifies data and can have side effects. PUT replaces resources (also idempotent). DELETE removes resources. Only GET is designed purely for safe retrieval without modifications.

questions.q: What does an HTTP 404 status code indicate?
options: The requested resource was not found on the server
The server encountered an internal error while processing the request
The client sent an invalid request syntax
The resource exists but access is forbidden
questions.explanation: 404 Not Found means the server received a valid request but cannot locate the requested resource. 500 is a server error, 400 is bad request syntax, and 403 is forbidden access. These are distinct client-side vs. server-side failures.

questions.q: How do HTTP cookies help maintain user sessions?
options: The server sets a cookie in the client's browser, which the client includes in subsequent requests, allowing the server to recognize the user without re-authentication
Cookies encrypt all HTTP traffic between client and server
Cookies allow the client to request multiple resources in parallel
Cookies store the user's password securely for automatic login
questions.explanation: Cookies are small pieces of data stored on the client that the browser automatically sends with each request to the same domain. The server can use a session ID in a cookie to maintain state. Cookies themselves do not encrypt traffic (HTTPS does) and should never store passwords directly.

questions.q: What is a key principle of REST (Representational State Transfer) architecture?
options: Resources are identified by URIs and manipulated using standard HTTP methods; the server provides hypermedia to guide clients
All requests must be made in JSON format with custom headers
REST requires a persistent connection between client and server
Resources are accessed through a single endpoint that accepts query parameters
questions.explanation: REST uses HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) on resource URIs, with the server providing hypermedia links for discoverability. REST does not mandate JSON (could use XML or other formats), is stateless (no persistent connection required), and typically uses multiple endpoints per resource. A single endpoint with query parameters is RPC-style, not REST.

questions.q: Which HTTP method should be used to partially update a resource?
options: PATCH applies partial modifications to an existing resource
PUT replaces the entire resource with new content
POST creates a new resource at a specified location
DELETE removes the resource and returns a 204 No Content response
questions.explanation: PATCH is designed for partial updates and only sends the fields that changed. PUT replaces the entire resource, requiring the client to send the complete new version. POST typically creates new resources. DELETE removes resources entirely. PATCH is appropriate when you want to update just some fields.

questions.q: What does the HTTP Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header accomplish?
options: It tells the browser to always use HTTPS for future connections to that domain, preventing downgrade attacks
It enables HTTP/2 multiplexing for faster loading
It allows the server to set domain-specific cookies
It compresses the HTTP response body using gzip
questions.explanation: HSTS (via Strict-Transport-Security header) forces browsers to use HTTPS only, preventing attackers from downgrading to plain HTTP. It's a security header that mitigates protocol downgrade attacks. HTTP/2 is a separate upgrade, cookies are handled via Set-Cookie, and compression uses Content-Encoding.

questions.q: In the TLS/SSL handshake for HTTPS, what is the purpose of the cipher suite negotiation?
options: The client and server agree on encryption algorithms, hash functions, and key exchange methods they both support
The client encrypts its password using a cipher selected by the server
The server sends its private key to the client for encryption
The handshake determines the HTTP version to use (HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2)
questions.explanation: During the TLS handshake, the client and server exchange supported cipher suites and mutually select one that both support. This ensures compatible encryption and authentication mechanisms. The server never sends its private key; the client never needs it. HTTP version negotiation (ALPN) happens separately during or after the TLS handshake.

questions.q: What is the purpose of the HTTP Content-Type header?
options: It specifies the media type of the resource body, allowing the client to correctly interpret and display the content
It sets the maximum size of the request body that the server will accept
It indicates how long the client should cache the response
It specifies the authentication method required for the resource
questions.explanation: Content-Type (e.g., 'application/json', 'text/html') tells the client the format of the response body so it can parse it correctly. Content-Length specifies body size, Cache-Control handles caching, and Authorization/WWW-Authenticate handle authentication.

questions.q: What is the difference between HTTP status codes 301 and 302?
options: 301 (Moved Permanently) indicates a permanent redirect that browsers cache, while 302 (Found) indicates a temporary redirect that browsers do not cache
301 is faster than 302 because it uses an optimized protocol
302 is more secure because it does not reveal the final URL to the client
301 requires client confirmation while 302 happens automatically
questions.explanation: 301 is a permanent redirect cached by browsers, so future requests go directly to the new URL. 302 is temporary; the client should keep using the original URL. Both happen automatically without client confirmation. Neither has inherent speed or security advantages over the other; they differ in intent (permanent vs. temporary).
